aviation.title: Aviation English
aviation.subtitle: ICAO Level 4-6 Standard
aviation.description: Master ICAO-standard English for pilots, air traffic controllers, and aviation professionals. Covers phraseology, emergency procedures, and standard communications.

vocabulary.example: Roger, maintaining flight level 350.
Wilco, turning to heading 270.
Affirm, we have the ATIS information.
Negative, unable to climb at this time.
Stand by, checking the weather conditions.
Say again your last transmission.
Correction, our fuel state is 5.5 hours.
Go ahead, ready to copy clearance.

lessons.title: Departure Clearance
lessons.text: Avianca 801, cleared to Miami via DOTEL departure, maintain runway heading until 3000 feet, then direct DOTEL. Climb and maintain flight level 310. Squawk 2341. Departure frequency 119.1.

questions.question: What altitude should they climb to initially?

lessons.title: Emergency Communications
prompts: Mayday, Mayday, Mayday. Engine failure. Requesting immediate return to field.
Pan Pan, Pan Pan, Pan Pan. Medical emergency on board. Request priority landing.
Request immediate descent due to cabin pressure loss.
We are declaring an emergency. Low fuel state. Minimum fuel.
Request vectors to nearest suitable airport. One engine inoperative.

vocabulary.example: Weather is CAVOK, no significant clouds.
Moderate turbulence reported at FL200.
Visibility 800 meters in fog.
Thunderstorm 20 miles north of the field.
Wind shear reported on final approach.
Entering IMC, switching to instruments.
The aircraft must maintain its airworthiness certificate.
Technician reported a hydraulic snag.
Aircraft cleared for dispatch after inspection.
Engine ground run required after maintenance.
Certifying engineer must sign off the log.
AOG situation — need parts urgently.

medical.title: Medical English
medical.subtitle: Clinical & Healthcare Communication
medical.description: Professional medical English for doctors, nurses, and healthcare workers. Patient communication, clinical documentation, and international medical protocols.

vocabulary.example: What is your chief complaint today?
When was the onset of your symptoms?
The patient has a chronic heart condition.
Acute pain in the lower abdomen.
The prognosis for this condition is good.
The diagnosis is type 2 diabetes.
Aspirin is contraindicated for children.
The dosage is 500mg twice daily.

lessons.title: Emergency Room Scenarios
prompts: Explain CPR procedure to a colleague.
Describe the symptoms of a myocardial infarction.
Inform a patient about their surgery preparation.
Report a patient's vital signs to a senior doctor.
Explain medication side effects to a patient.

lessons.title: Reading: Clinical Case Study
lessons.text: A 58-year-old male patient presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of severe chest pain radiating to the left arm, onset 2 hours ago. The patient reports diaphoresis and shortness of breath. Medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications: metformin 1000mg BD, lisinopril 10mg OD. Physical examination reveals BP 160/95, HR 98 bpm, RR 22/min, SpO2 94% on room air. ECG shows ST-elevation in leads II, III, and aVF, consistent with inferior STEMI. Patient is administered aspirin 300mg, clopidogrel 600mg, and heparin IV. Urgent catheterization laboratory activation was initiated.

questions.question: What was the patient's oxygen saturation?

business.title: Business English
business.subtitle: Corporate & Professional Communication
business.description: Master corporate communication, negotiations, presentations, emails, and boardroom English. Essential for executives, managers, and business professionals.

exercises.question: Which opening is most formal for a cold business email?
Which closing phrase is most appropriate for a formal email?

exercises.sentence: I ___ writing to inquire about your services.

exercises.question: What does 'Please revert at your earliest convenience' mean?

vocabulary.example: The bottom line is we need 15% discount.
Give me a ballpark figure for the project.
Negotiations reached a deadlock on pricing.
They rejected our offer and made a counter-offer.
Let's find a win-win solution.
We need to complete due diligence before signing.
Please sign the NDA before we proceed.
All stakeholders must approve the merger.

lessons.title: Boardroom Presentations
prompts: Present your Q3 financial results to the board.
Pitch a new product idea to investors.
Handle a difficult question during a presentation.
Open a business meeting professionally.
Summarize action items at the end of a meeting.

lessons.title: Reading: Annual Report Analysis
lessons.text: For the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, TechCorp Inc. reported record revenues of $2.8 billion, representing a 34% year-over-year increase. Gross margin improved to 68%, driven by favorable product mix and operational efficiencies. EBITDA reached $840 million, with EBITDA margin expanding by 280 basis points to 30%. The company's cloud segment emerged as the primary growth driver, contributing 52% of total revenue, up from 38% in the prior year. Cash and cash equivalents stood at $1.2 billion, providing ample liquidity for strategic investments and potential M&A opportunities. The board of directors approved a $500 million share buyback program, reflecting confidence in the company's long-term value creation potential.

questions.question: What was the revenue growth rate year-over-year?
What does 'EBITDA' stand for in the context?
options: Earnings Before Income Tax, Depreciation and Amortization
Earnings Before Interest, Tax, Depreciation and Amortization
Estimated Business Income, Tax, Dividends and Assets
Executive Budget for Internal Tax and Depreciation Analysis

questions.question: What is the primary growth driver?

technology.title: Tech & IT English
technology.subtitle: Software, DevOps & Engineering
technology.description: Technical English for software engineers, DevOps, data scientists, and IT professionals. Code reviews, documentation, standups, and technical presentations.

vocabulary.example: We need to refactor this legacy code.
We'll deploy the new version to production tonight.
I'm debugging the authentication module.
The database query is the main bottleneck.
We have significant tech debt in the payments module.
Share your blockers in the daily standup.
Please review my pull request before merging.
We'll tackle this feature in the next sprint.

lessons.title: Code Review Communication
prompts: Explain why you chose a microservices architecture.
Describe a recent bug you fixed and how you found it.
Propose a solution to a performance problem.
Explain the difference between REST and GraphQL APIs.
Present your team's sprint retrospective findings.

engineering.title: Engineering English
engineering.subtitle: Technical & Industrial Communication
engineering.description: Precision English for mechanical, electrical, civil, and industrial engineers. Technical documentation, project management, and international collaboration.

vocabulary.example: The tolerance for this component is ±0.01mm.
This is a load-bearing wall — do not remove.
Instruments require regular calibration.
Check the technical specification sheet.
The prototype passed all stress tests.
Steel has a high yield strength.

customer_service.title: Customer Service English
customer_service.subtitle: Call Centers & Client Relations
customer_service.description: Professional customer-facing English for call centers, helpdesks, hospitality, and retail. Handling complaints, de-escalation, and building rapport.

vocabulary.example: I completely understand your frustration.
I'll escalate this to our specialist team.
We'll work toward a satisfactory resolution.
We'd like to compensate you with a full refund.
I'll follow up with you within 24 hours.
Our return policy allows 30 days.

lessons.title: Role Play: Angry Customer
prompts: Apologize to a customer whose order arrived late.
Explain your company's refund policy clearly.
De-escalate a very angry customer on the phone.
Offer alternatives when a product is out of stock.
Close a support call professionally and positively.

stories.title: A Day in New York
stories.description: Follow María's first day in New York City as she navigates the city, meets new people, and discovers American culture.

scenes.text: María arrives at JFK airport. She looks for the subway.

dialogue.speaker: María
dialogue.text: Excuse me, how do I get to Manhattan?

dialogue.speaker: Stranger
dialogue.text: Take the AirTrain to Jamaica, then the E train to Midtown.

dialogue.speaker: María
dialogue.text: How long does it take?

dialogue.speaker: Stranger
dialogue.text: About 45 minutes. It's easy, don't worry!

question.text: How does María travel to Manhattan?

scenes.text: María checks into her hotel in Times Square.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Welcome to the Grand Hotel! Do you have a reservation?

dialogue.speaker: María
dialogue.text: Yes, my name is María García. I booked a single room for three nights.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Perfect! Here's your key card. Breakfast is included from 7 to 10 AM.

dialogue.speaker: María
dialogue.text: Thank you very much! Is there a gym in the hotel?

scenes.text: María orders food at a New York deli.

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.text: What can I get for you today?

dialogue.speaker: María
dialogue.text: I'd like a turkey club sandwich, please.

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.text: What kind of bread? We have rye, sourdough, or whole wheat.

dialogue.speaker: María
dialogue.text: Sourdough, please. And can I have it toasted?

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.text: Absolutely! Anything to drink?

stories.title: The Job Interview
stories.description: Carlos prepares for and aces a job interview at a tech company in London.

scenes.text: Carlos receives an invitation for a job interview at TechCorp London.

dialogue.speaker: HR Email
dialogue.text: Dear Carlos, we'd like to invite you for a Technical Interview on Tuesday at 10 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.text: Dear Ms. Johnson, thank you for the opportunity. I confirm my attendance for Tuesday at 10 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.text: Good morning, Carlos. I'm Sarah, Head of Engineering. Pleasure to meet you.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.text: Good morning, Sarah. The pleasure is mine. Thank you for having me.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.text: Tell me a bit about yourself and why you're interested in this role.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.text: I'm a full-stack developer with 5 years of experience. I'm passionate about scalable systems and I believe this role aligns perfectly with my career goals.

stories.title: Medical Emergency
stories.description: Dr. Rodriguez handles a critical emergency in a US hospital, communicating with an international team.

scenes.text: A critical patient arrives at the ER.

dialogue.speaker: Paramedic
dialogue.text: Male, 62, found unresponsive. BP 80/40, heart rate 120 and thready. Possible cardiogenic shock.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Rodriguez
dialogue.text: Let's get him on the monitor. Large bore IV access, two lines. Order ECG and troponins stat.

dialogue.speaker: Nurse
dialogue.text: IV access established. ECG showing ST changes in anterior leads.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Rodriguez
dialogue.text: Activate the cath lab. This is a STEMI. Get cardiology on the phone immediately.

question.text: What does 'stat' mean in medical English?

miniGames.description: Translate as many words as possible before time runs out

dailyChallenges.description: Complete all grammar exercises without mistakes
